metadata.title: Less Fragmented Than We Thought? Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication Science, 2010–2019

abstract: With the explosive growth in research topics, communication science is said to be more fragmented and hyper-specialized than ever before, producing an increasing number of small, niche research topics that lack intellectual coherence as a whole. While such issues have been a central concern for the field, there has been a relative lack of systematic effort to map the topical interconnections among different communication science subfields, answering the question of how they remain empirically fragmented. Using full-texts of scholarly articles published in the top 20 communication science journals from 2010 to 2019, we provide systematic evidence to such claims in terms of their actual contents and their connectivity patterns. Drawing on extant works concerning the sociology of science and structures of scientific knowledge, as well as on topic modeling and simulation-based inferences on network topological features, we find that subdisciplinary linkage in communication is more frequent than we often think.

paragraphs: core set of knowledge and analytical frameworks that distinguishes communication science from other disciplines? Indeed, given its lack of intellectual coherence as a whole, "the nature of the discipline often remains unclear" (Nordenstreng, 2007, p. 211), even after almost 70 years of its existence. As such, those fundamental ontological questions have long constituted a recurring theme in meta-knowledge about communication science as an academic field (e.g., Berelson, 1959;Craig, 1999;Donsbach, 2006;Herbst, 2008;Rogers, 1994).

Communication science has long been characterized as the "multiplicity of theoretical, conceptual and empirical fragments" (Journal of Communication Call for Special Issue, 2019). With the explosive growth in research topics, communication science is said to be more fragmented and hyper-specialized than ever before (e.g., see Waisbord (2019) for the latest discussion of this issue). However, the issue of intellectual diversification and increasing specialization is not entirely new (see Herbst, 2008;Rogers, 1994;Walter, Cody, & Ball-Rokeach, 2018) nor unique to our discipline. For instance, there have been many similar observations and commentaries regarding the "fractured" status of a discipline in a wide range of social sciences, including economics (Davis, 2019), psychology (Zittoun, Gillespie, & Cornish, 2009), political science (Garand, 2005;Goodin & Klingemann, 1996), and sociology (Leahey & Moody, 2014;Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006). Nevertheless, there seems to be an even more persistent disenchantment with such trends in our field (Berelson, 1959;Donsbach, 2006;Nordenstreng, 2007).

Although discussion on such issues remains far from being resolved, this debate raises several important-yet seldom discussed-issues regarding the nature of diversification trends within the field: What are the subfields of communication science, and how do we define such divisions? How closely-or, in contrast, looselyare various subdisciplines of communication science interconnected? Indeed, how "fragmented" are subdisciplines within communication science? Numerous prior observations and commentaries have been made regarding the disciplinary status of the field as a whole. However, as we shall elaborate below, these studies have not directly investigated the issue of classifying different subdisciplines within the field (e.g., Barnett et al., 2011;Walter et al., 2018;Zhu & Fu, 2019), nor examined their empirical (dis)connection (e.g., Gu ¨nther & Domahidi, 2017;Lim, Park, & Baek, 2019), yielding incomplete evidence of the field's fragmentation.

We take this opportunity to take a step back for a moment and reflect the broader assumption defining the so-called "fragmentation thesis": that the field is increasingly fragmented along the lines of different niches and subdisciplines. Underlying this argument is a set of propositions that (a) communication science can be broken down into several "distinctive clusters" of research topics and questions that define "subdisciplines," and that (b) such different subdisciplines are not at all well-connected to one another. Therefore, the field as a whole remains severely fractured while it lacks a core body of knowledge. We attempt to provide systematic, empirical evidence to such claims by examining the actual contents of what communication scientists write and publish in the discipline's top journals. Further, we investigate how subdisciplines in communication science are empirically interrelated-in terms of their connectivity patterns-across the lines of different research topics and questions. Moreover, through drawing on extant works concerning the sociology of science and structures of scientific knowledge, we argue that subdisciplinary linkage in communication is more frequent than we often think.
The "fragmentation thesis" and communication as a multidisciplinary field

From the perspective of the sociology of science, the word "discipline" refers to a distinctive branch of knowledge that is defined by a core subject area with wellestablished research questions and analytical approaches. Discrete disciplinary boundaries have long represented the default structure of scientific knowledge production ("Mode 1" production: Gibbons et al., 1994; also see Kuhn, 1962). However, with growing demand for application-specific knowledge of complex social issues, scientific knowledge is increasingly driven by multiple, heterogeneous academic disciplines ("Mode 2" production: Gibbons et al., 1994; also see Choi & Pak, 2006). In this regard, an "interdisciplinary" field is a field of study that spans and integrates two or more discrete disciplines, and is often deemed to produce novel and new insights that otherwise cannot be produced by a single discipline alone (Newman, 2001). Consequently, an increasing push toward interdisciplinary research has become evident across numerous social science fields (Dogan, 1996;Herbst, 2008).

Many historical reviews and commentaries of the origin, institutionalization, and development of communication science indicate that our discipline has been propelled by a set of applied questions and societal concerns, especially during and following the World War II period (see Berger, Roloff, & Roskos-Ewoldsen, 2010;Waisbord, 2019). As "one of the greatest crossroads" (Schramm et al., 1959, p. 8), communication science has therefore been "stunningly interdisciplinary" from the start (Herbst, 2008, p. 604). Nevertheless, unlike truly "interdisciplinary" research in the sense of aiming to integrate and synthesize existing disciplinary boundaries (Choi & Pak, 2006;Klein, 2010), communication science has often been characterized as highly "divisive" and "fragmented" in nature (Waisbord, 2019).

In his most up-to-date observation of the field's disciplinary status, Waisbord (2019) attributes the root cause of fragmentation in the field to a long-standing ontological disagreement with respect to the meaning of "communication" across various perspectives within the field. Echoing Shepherd (1993), Craig (1999), and Jime ´nez & Guillem (2009)-all of whom propose that a particular "discipline" offers a specific view of being-he asserts that the communication discipline lacks a clear, shared understanding of "communication." Unlike more established disciplines such as sociology (which studies "society"), psychology (the "human mind"), or political science ("power" and "politics"), different traditions in communication studies offer widely contrasting understandings of "communication" as the subject of a study and the analytical focus of an inquiry. For instance, whereas the semiotic tradition regards communication as the intersubjective creation of "meanings" by signs (therefore focusing on messages), the cybernetic tradition primarily describes communication as the functional "transmission" of information and its processing systems as a whole. In contrast, the sociopsychological tradition sees communication as the interactions among individuals and their influence (i.e., patterns of communication and their effects), whereas the sociocultural tradition treats communication as the (re)production of "order" and "power" (for a more detailed discussion on this issue, see Craig (1999) and Waisbord (2019)). In essence, Waisbord's (2019) bird's-eye view suggests that communication science is multidisciplinary rather than interdisciplinary, 1 as it "draws on knowledge from different disciplines but stays within the boundaries of those [different] fields" (Choi & Pak, 2006, p. 359). Communication science is said to have achieved the status of a "post-discipline" (Waisbord, 2019, p. 127), where many middle-range theories and specialized research questions are sought by multiple, pragmatic approaches without necessarily being integrated.

sections.header_raw: "Fragmentation" and topological structures of scientific knowledge production
sections.header_norm: fragmentation and topological structures of scientific knowledge production
paragraphs: Traditionally, such trends toward specialization and diversification within our field-and its mirror image of a lack of theoretical core-have long been considered detrimental. Indeed, it is widely believed that a greater division across subdisciplines may lead to the isolation and alienation of scholars of different specialties (Wiemann et al., 1988), precluding broader theoretical integration (Craig, 1999), and possibly even leading to the demise of the discipline itself (Berelson, 1959;Donsbach, 2006;Rogers, 1994).

While we take no definitive stance on this issue, it seems that many commentaries regarding the "ideal state" of the discipline rely on the implicit model of "normal" science (e.g., Kuhn, 1962; also see Zittoun, Gillespie, & Cornish, 2009). Although such a perspective rests upon an unrealistic (and therefore widely criticized) model of linear, consensual developments of scientific knowledge (Zittoun, Gillespie, & Cornish, 2009), it typically asserts that broader theoretical integration as a "core" is required, and that there should be consensual progress that defines the collective identity of the field. Such an "idealized" view of the field therefore evokes a particular topological structure that reflects centralized scientific knowledge accumulation. In contrast, a sharp subdisciplinary division without apparent theoretical cores-an essential structure of "multidisciplinarity" as in many of the field's commentaries-may exhibit a considerably different topological structure. Following the seminal work of Moody (2004) and others (Carolan, 2008;Moody & Light, 2006) who have formalized this notion into testable expectations, we link the fragmentation (or lack thereof) within the field of communication science with a particular structure of scientific knowledge.

In what follows, we briefly present four distinct-yet not mutually exclusive-"prototypical" images of the structure of the communication research field. These topologies, all schematically represented in Figure 1, are chosen mainly based on two considerations: First, each of these topologies has been extensively studied with regard to its structural properties (Moody and White, 2003 ;Uzzi et al., 2007 ;Watts & Strogatz, 1998), and each of which has considerable theoretical relevance to the different "images" of a field's disciplinary status. Second, these topologies have been frequently employed in prior studies concerning other "low-consensus" disciplines, such as sociology (e.g., Moody, 2004) or education research (e.g., Carolan, 2008). This provides a good reference point against which we can further (albeit indirectly) compare and contextualize our findings. While we acknowledge that the four topologies presented here are by no means exhaustive, they nevertheless help us to systematically conceptualize the particular structure implied by the so-called "fragmentation thesis" and its counterparts.

A scale-free network versus a fragmented network A well-defined scientific core with an agreed-upon theoretical consensus and methodological approaches-the structure of a traditional disciplinary core model-may exhibit a high degree of a core-periphery or "star-like" structure in its knowledge (e.g., Hill & Carley, 1999;Merton, 1968;Moody, 2004). In this model, there exists a single (or a few) conceptual core(s), to which most ideas and research topics are connected. In contrast, the other research topics are only indirectly connected with one another. This scale-free network structure (Baraba ´si & Albert, 1999;Newman, 2001), as depicted in Figure 1a, signals a higher degree of centralization in knowledge accumulation within a field.

In comparison, one of the defining characteristics of a multidisciplinary field is that it is often subdivided into different clusters of research areas, or subdisciplines. "With networks of researchers who themselves develop boundaries with other specializations within the field" (MacInnis & Folkes, 2009, p. 908), this may be characterized as a highly fragmented and plural field, as depicted in Figure 1b. In this network, ideas and theoretical innovations are largely restricted within each archipelago of a narrowly defined subfield, and are rarely spread across different specialty areas (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004). 2 Structural cohesion and cross-fertilization Some assert that an increasing degree of specialization and the rise of niche subdisciplines does not necessarily lead to undesirable consequences (Dogan, 1996;Leahey & Reikowsky, 2008). That is, an increasing trend of specialization and the development of multiple subdisciplines may instead indicate disciplinary maturity. Increased specialization is accompanied by a "hybridization" of different special subfields, which in turn leads to a recombination and re-structuration of traditional boundaries (Dogan, 1996). Termed the structural cohesion model by Moody (2004; also see Leahey & Moody, 2014), this model suggests that a lack of well-defined boundaries renders it possible to easily borrow, import, apply, and advance certain perspectives from neighboring (sub-)disciplines. Such a model can therefore be characterized as the relative "even-mixing" of different areas (Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006), as described in Figure 1c. Despite the lack of sharp division or apparent "core" of a field (Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006), a structurally cohesive knowledge structure provides a stable structure of connections among ideas, research topics, and collaboration patterns (e.g., Carolan, 2008).

A small-world network A final network topology-the small-world model (Watts, 1999;Watts & Strogatz, 1998) as described in Figure 1d-offers a means of harmonizing specialization (e.g., differentiation) and broader integration. A small-world structure is characterized as the combination of two characteristics that are normally divergent (Watts, 1999): a high degree of local clustering (which signals well-defined communities) and a small number of average steps among all pairs of nodes (signaling a high degree of integration). The key difference between the small-world and the plural world (i.e., fragmented) models, according to Carolan (2008) and Newman (2001), is therefore the ability of any given node to quickly reach another in the network without sacrificing the internal cohesion of the local cluster. This is enabled by the presence of a small number of nodes that intersect across distinct research areas, where such nodes "become many things to many different others, . . . offering a little something for lots of people" (Carolan, 2008, p. 72). This provides an avenue for the use of novel theoretical progress in one cluster to be productively utilized in another, increasing the potential for new discoveries (Granovetter, 1983;Newman, 2001;Moody & White, 2003;Uzzi & Spiro, 2005).

How fragmented are we actually? Subdisciplines and their (dis)integration Following Leahey and Moody ( 2014), here we tentatively define a subdiscipline as internal divisions (such as subfields) within a single academic discipline. Akin to different academic fields, a subdiscipline produces loosely distinctive ideas, topics, and subject matter knowledge that are "largely distinguished by their substantive focus of inquiry" (Leahey & Moody, 2014, p. 230), albeit to a lesser extent than disciplinary divisions.

Often, the claim that the field of communication science is highly divided across many subdisciplines and niche specialty areas is taken for granted. We do not necessarily disagree with such commentaries, especially in that such accounts provide useful heuristic representations of rich, diverse fields of communication studies, as well as in that they reflect experiences of ourselves as communication scholars. However, we argue that it is also true that systematic empirical evidence to support such claims remains rather limited.

First, many prior observations and commentaries rely on an impressionisticand often excessively simplistic-dichotomous division that artificially divides extant studies. Historically, many different criteria have been proposed, a nonexhaustive list of which includes platform/channel differences (e.g., mass vs. CMC vs. interpersonal, etc.), levels of analysis (e.g., micro vs. macro), applied/thematic contexts (e.g., health vs. political vs. science, etc.), the media "effect" vs. "production," or epistemological and methodological (e.g., critical vs. empirical) differences (see, for instance: Berger et al., 2010;Craig, 1999;Donsbach, 2006;Rogers, 1994). While we agree that such divisions do exist, we also concur with Waisbord's (2019, p. 49) observation that "[communication scholarship] does not lend itself to be neatly classified in well-defined boxes." Indeed, the sheer number of different dimensions of dividing up communication scholarship (quite paradoxically) indicates that although there seems to be a broad agreement regarding the existence of the fragmentation of the field, there is no agreed-upon division that clearly defines such different subdisciplines, nor is there a sharp "divide" across these subdisciplines. In this regard, existing (often overly simplified) schemes of subdisciplines might risk a researcher's confirmation bias.

Second, a number of more quantitatively oriented studies have examined connections within and across various social science disciplines (e.g., Barnett et al., 2011;Park & Leydesdorff, 2009;Walter et al., 2018). These studies typically focus on the co-citation patterns of each journal or article (i.e., a bibliometric analysis). However, using a (co-) citation to establish a linkage pattern across subdisciplines is especially problematic in this context. As a proxy measure of how particular concepts and topics are being used and transferred across different articles/journals, citation patterns can be shaped by many factors beyond intellectual connections (e.g., reputation, personal connections, or even the length of the title: see Bramoulle ´& Ductor, 2018;Gondal, 2011). Nor is every concept or idea clearly accompanied by citations. Moreover, they tend to fail to consider the actual contents of journal articles, while classifying each journal/article into broad but simple categories (e.g., communication vs. psychology vs. political science, etc.), assuming that all journal/ articles in a given category are conceptually similar to one another. This provides much incomplete evidence of actual "subdiscipline" fragmentation within the field.

Lastly and recently, there has been a more systematic attempt to map the diversity of research topics and extant theories, directly based on what communication scholars write about in reality (e.g., Gu ¨nther & Domahidi, 2017;Lim, Park, & Baek, 2019). Traditionally, such trends toward specialization and diversification within our field-and its mirror image of a lack of theoretical core-have long been considered detrimental. Indeed, it is widely believed that a greater division across subdisciplines may lead to the isolation and alienation of scholars of different specialties (Wiemann et al., 1988), precluding broader theoretical integration (Craig, 1999), and possibly even leading to the demise of the discipline itself (Berelson, 1959;Donsbach, 2006;Rogers, 1994).

While we take no definitive stance on this issue, it seems that many commentaries regarding the "ideal state" of the discipline rely on the implicit model of "normal" science (e.g., Kuhn, 1962; also see Zittoun, Gillespie, & Cornish, 2009). Although such a perspective rests upon an unrealistic (and therefore widely criticized) model of linear, consensual developments of scientific knowledge (Zittoun, Gillespie, & Cornish, 2009), it typically asserts that broader theoretical integration as a "core" is required, and that there should be consensual progress that defines the collective identity of the field. Such an "idealized" view of the field therefore evokes a particular topological structure that reflects centralized scientific knowledge accumulation. In contrast, a sharp subdisciplinary division without apparent theoretical cores-an essential structure of "multidisciplinarity" as in many of the field's commentaries-may exhibit a considerably different topological structure. Following the seminal work of Moody (2004) and others (Carolan, 2008;Moody & Light, 2006) who have formalized this notion into testable expectations, we link the fragmentation (or lack thereof) within the field of communication science with a particular structure of scientific knowledge.

In what follows, we briefly present four distinct-yet not mutually exclusive-"prototypical" images of the structure of the communication research field. These topologies, all schematically represented in Figure 1, are chosen mainly based on two considerations: First, each of these topologies has been extensively studied with regard to its structural properties (Moody and White, 2003 ;Uzzi et al., 2007 ;Watts & Strogatz, 1998), and each of which has considerable theoretical relevance to the different "images" of a field's disciplinary status. Second, these topologies have been frequently employed in prior studies concerning other "low-consensus" disciplines, such as sociology (e.g., Moody, 2004) or education research (e.g., Carolan, 2008). This provides a good reference point against which we can further (albeit indirectly) compare and contextualize our findings. While we acknowledge that the four topologies presented here are by no means exhaustive, they nevertheless help us to systematically conceptualize the particular structure implied by the so-called "fragmentation thesis" and its counterparts.

A scale-free network versus a fragmented network A well-defined scientific core with an agreed-upon theoretical consensus and methodological approaches-the structure of a traditional disciplinary core model-may exhibit a high degree of a core-periphery or "star-like" structure in its knowledge (e.g., Hill & Carley, 1999;Merton, 1968;Moody, 2004). In this model, there exists a single (or a few) conceptual core(s), to which most ideas and research topics are connected. In contrast, the other research topics are only indirectly connected with one another. This scale-free network structure (Baraba ´si & Albert, 1999;Newman, 2001), as depicted in Figure 1a, signals a higher degree of centralization in knowledge accumulation within a field.

In comparison, one of the defining characteristics of a multidisciplinary field is that it is often subdivided into different clusters of research areas, or subdisciplines. "With networks of researchers who themselves develop boundaries with other specializations within the field" (MacInnis & Folkes, 2009, p. 908), this may be characterized as a highly fragmented and plural field, as depicted in Figure 1b. In this network, ideas and theoretical innovations are largely restricted within each archipelago of a narrowly defined subfield, and are rarely spread across different specialty areas (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004). 2 Structural cohesion and cross-fertilization Some assert that an increasing degree of specialization and the rise of niche subdisciplines does not necessarily lead to undesirable consequences (Dogan, 1996;Leahey & Reikowsky, 2008). That is, an increasing trend of specialization and the development of multiple subdisciplines may instead indicate disciplinary maturity. Increased specialization is accompanied by a "hybridization" of different special subfields, which in turn leads to a recombination and re-structuration of traditional boundaries (Dogan, 1996). Termed the structural cohesion model by Moody (2004; also see Leahey & Moody, 2014), this model suggests that a lack of well-defined boundaries renders it possible to easily borrow, import, apply, and advance certain perspectives from neighboring (sub-)disciplines. Such a model can therefore be characterized as the relative "even-mixing" of different areas (Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006), as described in Figure 1c. Despite the lack of sharp division or apparent "core" of a field (Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006), a structurally cohesive knowledge structure provides a stable structure of connections among ideas, research topics, and collaboration patterns (e.g., Carolan, 2008).

A small-world network A final network topology-the small-world model (Watts, 1999;Watts & Strogatz, 1998) as described in Figure 1d-offers a means of harmonizing specialization (e.g., differentiation) and broader integration. A small-world structure is characterized as the combination of two characteristics that are normally divergent (Watts, 1999): a high degree of local clustering (which signals well-defined communities) and a small number of average steps among all pairs of nodes (signaling a high degree of integration). The key difference between the small-world and the plural world (i.e., fragmented) models, according to Carolan (2008) and Newman (2001), is therefore the ability of any given node to quickly reach another in the network without sacrificing the internal cohesion of the local cluster. This is enabled by the presence of a small number of nodes that intersect across distinct research areas, where such nodes "become many things to many different others, . . . offering a little something for lots of people" (Carolan, 2008, p. 72). This provides an avenue for the use of novel theoretical progress in one cluster to be productively utilized in another, increasing the potential for new discoveries (Granovetter, 1983;Newman, 2001;Moody & White, 2003;Uzzi & Spiro, 2005).

How fragmented are we actually? Subdisciplines and their (dis)integration Following Leahey and Moody ( 2014), here we tentatively define a subdiscipline as internal divisions (such as subfields) within a single academic discipline. Akin to different academic fields, a subdiscipline produces loosely distinctive ideas, topics, and subject matter knowledge that are "largely distinguished by their substantive focus of inquiry" (Leahey & Moody, 2014, p. 230), albeit to a lesser extent than disciplinary divisions.

Often, the claim that the field of communication science is highly divided across many subdisciplines and niche specialty areas is taken for granted. We do not necessarily disagree with such commentaries, especially in that such accounts provide useful heuristic representations of rich, diverse fields of communication studies, as well as in that they reflect experiences of ourselves as communication scholars. However, we argue that it is also true that systematic empirical evidence to support such claims remains rather limited.

First, many prior observations and commentaries rely on an impressionisticand often excessively simplistic-dichotomous division that artificially divides extant studies. Historically, many different criteria have been proposed, a nonexhaustive list of which includes platform/channel differences (e.g., mass vs. CMC vs. interpersonal, etc.), levels of analysis (e.g., micro vs. macro), applied/thematic contexts (e.g., health vs. political vs. science, etc.), the media "effect" vs. "production," or epistemological and methodological (e.g., critical vs. empirical) differences (see, for instance: Berger et al., 2010;Craig, 1999;Donsbach, 2006;Rogers, 1994). While we agree that such divisions do exist, we also concur with Waisbord's (2019, p. 49) observation that "[communication scholarship] does not lend itself to be neatly classified in well-defined boxes." Indeed, the sheer number of different dimensions of dividing up communication scholarship (quite paradoxically) indicates that although there seems to be a broad agreement regarding the existence of the fragmentation of the field, there is no agreed-upon division that clearly defines such different subdisciplines, nor is there a sharp "divide" across these subdisciplines. In this regard, existing (often overly simplified) schemes of subdisciplines might risk a researcher's confirmation bias.

Second, a number of more quantitatively oriented studies have examined connections within and across various social science disciplines (e.g., Barnett et al., 2011;Park & Leydesdorff, 2009;Walter et al., 2018). These studies typically focus on the co-citation patterns of each journal or article (i.e., a bibliometric analysis). However, using a (co-) citation to establish a linkage pattern across subdisciplines is especially problematic in this context. As a proxy measure of how particular concepts and topics are being used and transferred across different articles/journals, citation patterns can be shaped by many factors beyond intellectual connections (e.g., reputation, personal connections, or even the length of the title: see Bramoulle ´& Ductor, 2018;Gondal, 2011). Nor is every concept or idea clearly accompanied by citations. Moreover, they tend to fail to consider the actual contents of journal articles, while classifying each journal/article into broad but simple categories (e.g., communication vs. psychology vs. political science, etc.), assuming that all journal/ articles in a given category are conceptually similar to one another. This provides much incomplete evidence of actual "subdiscipline" fragmentation within the field.

sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: In order to answer these research questions, we examine scholarly articles that appeared in top communication science journals (journal N ¼ 20) from 2010 to 2019 (total article N ¼ 8,819). We chose this time frame (from 2010 to 2019) since our method requires us to parse html versions of the full-texts of scholarly articles, which only became available around 2010 for most of the journals we are targeting here (also see footnote 5 for details). Utilizing a topic modeling method (Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003;Roberts et al., 2014; also see Gu ¨nther & Domahidi (2017) for a similar approach), our approach here is to classify each article into a particular subdiscipline based on the actual "topics" of articles. Note that we use the term "topic" here to broadly denote the collection of words, ideas, and themes that tend to appear together in a meaningful way, following Gu ¨nther and Domahidi's (2017) recent contribution. This would provide a good approximation of the distinctive topics, approaches, and research questions that define the building blocks of different "subdisciplines" in communication science. In doing so, we also treat a certain cluster of topics as the approximation of ideas and subject matter knowledge that defines particular subdisciplines. Nevertheless, unlike prior research (e.g., Gu ¨nther & Domahidi, 2017; Lim, Park, & Baek, 2019), we make use of the full-text of published scholarly articles. This is based on the consideration that topic models that only utilize abstracts are known to underperform in terms of their understandability and interpretability relative to full-text-based analyses (Syed & Spruit, 2017;Syed & Weber, 2018). 3 In doing so, we fit a correlated topic model (hereafter denoted as CTM: Blei & Lafferty, 2006; also see Gu ¨nther & Domahidi (2017) for a similar application). As a recent extension of a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic model (Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003), CTM allows us to directly model the correlational structure among identified topics within a corpus, directly estimating the degree to which one topic appears together with another (termed a "correlation": Blei & Lafferty, 2006). This quantifies how the topics are correlated with one another, yielding direct evidence pertaining to the "fragmentation" of the fields.

Utilizing a topic modeling method (Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003;Roberts et al., 2014; also see Gu ¨nther & Domahidi (2017) for a similar approach), our approach here is to classify each article into a particular subdiscipline based on the actual "topics" of articles.
sections.preview_tail: This is based on the consideration that topic models that only utilize abstracts are known to underperform in terms of their understandability and interpretability relative to full-text-based analyses (Syed & Spruit, 2017;Syed & Weber, 2018). 3 In doing so, we fit a correlated topic model (hereafter denoted as CTM: Blei & Lafferty, 2006; also see Gu ¨nther & Domahidi (2017) for a similar application). As a recent extension of a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic model (Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003), CTM allows us to directly model the correlational structure among identified topics within a corpus, directly estimating the degree to which one topic appears together with another (termed a "correlation": Blei & Lafferty, 2006). This quantifies how the topics are correlated with one another, yielding direct evidence pertaining to the "fragmentation" of the fields.

sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: Based on the ISI Web of Science Journal Citation Report (JCR), we identified the abovementioned "top communication science journals" by first selecting journals listed as "Communication" (based on ISI designation), and then subsequently collecting any journals listed among the top 10 based on their impact factors at any time from 2010 to 2017 (the year in which the latest journal citation report was available at the time of data collection). 4 This should provide a broadly representative view of the state of our discipline. A list of journals and breakdowns of articles are presented in Table 1.

Based on the identified journals, we first scraped all metadata as well as the fulltext of each article published between 1 January 2010 and 30 April 2019 directly from the journal websites. This was to ensure that we would have the most up-todate published articles and their full-texts, as typical research databases such as EBSCOhost or ISI Web of Knowledge do not index "online first" or "ahead of print" articles or provide a full-text of an indexed article. 5 Out of a total of 10,695 returned entries, we excluded any duplicated entries as well as entries that were not original research articles (e.g., book reviews, reviewer acknowledgments, notices, etc.: excluded article N ¼ 1,876). Following general recommendations in the literature (e.g., Denny & Spirling, 2018;Maier et al., 2018;Roberts et al., 2014), we then cleaned any in-text citations or copyright-related markers from the remaining full-text, and then submitted the cleaned corpus to standard preprocessing steps. This involves (in order of preprocessing steps): lower-case conversion, removing stop words and certain highly commonly used terms in articles, removing punctuation and symbols, and lemmatization (see Online Appendix for details). Finally, we removed words that appear in more than 99.9% or less than 1% of the documents, based on adapted guidelines suggested by Maier et al. (2018). The final term-document matrix featured 8,819 unique documents and 27,799 unique words.

sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: While CTM relies on logistic normal distribution (rather than Dirichlet distribution) to model the latent topic proportions of a document, the model essentially maps each document as a multinomial distribution over K "topics," which are in turn a multinomial distribution over W words (Blei & Lafferty, 2006). However, this assumes that the number of latent topics is already known a priori, a nontrivial task in any topic modeling approach (Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004;Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). We followed a hybrid approach of combining standard recommendations suggested in the literature with a more data-driven approach (see Online Appendix subsequent analysis. 6 All of the analyses were based on R stm package version 1.3.3 (Roberts et al., 2014).

sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: Following the recent guidelines proposed by Maier et al. (2018), all three authors first independently judged and labeled the identified topics (topic N ¼ 110) based on top-word lists (using highest probability words as well as FREX score-based words) in relation to research topics and theories used in communication science. Those topics that were deemed irrelevant or uninterpretable (i.e., boilerplate topics, or topics with no substantive meanings: Mimno & Blei, 2011) were discarded from the results based on the collective discussion among the authors. Through this iterative process, a total of 106 topics were finally labeled, as presented in Table SA1 of Online Appendix. Furthermore, similarly to the inter-topic semantic validity (Maier et al., 2018), we additionally investigated their inter-topic relations by clustering the initially identified topics based on hierarchical agglomerative clustering (see Online Appendix for details). Such a method effectively separates labeled topics (K ¼ 106) into several distinctive "clusters," and is increasingly used to identify different dimensions in topic model solutions (e.g., Jiang et al., 2016;Yau et al., 2014).

Through jointly considering labeled topic solutions, topic clustering patterns, and domain expert knowledge, we collectively discussed whether the identified topics could be grouped together (hereafter referred to as "topic clusters"), and further, whether topic clusters could be assigned to a particular subdiscipline. 7 Based on this initial result, we further validated our initial subdiscipline designations against an expert survey, where we asked nine experts of the field (all assistant/ associate/full professors with varying expertise and specializations) individually to designate up to three most appropriate subdisciplines per topic cluster based on the top-word lists of manually labeled topics. Cross-tabulating our initial designations against the expert opinions, we resolved any discrepancy between the two by taking the majority rule in identifying the subdiscipline for topic clusters. Based on iterative processes, we combined the 106 initially labeled topics into 51 "topic clusters," with 16 different subdiscipline designations (including "no particular subdiscipline" where authors and experts could not agree upon a particular subdiscipline designation, or where the majority of authors and experts agreed that there was no particular subdiscipline). We manually removed some method clusters (Quantitative methods and Qualitative methods) from the subsequent analysis. 8 Below, we report the results of our analysis based on the final (validated) subdiscipline identifications, whose topic labels, topic clusters, and subdiscipline designations are presented in Figure 2 and Online Appendix Table SA1.

sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: Our RQ1 asked how different research topics and concepts are clustered together, essentially querying what the field of communication science research looks like.

Per our RQ1, we present a descriptive overview of the status of the discipline, as shown in Figure 2. From the estimated topic document (h) matrix, we first reduced the full matrix to a more condensed form by grouping topics within identified "topic clusters" as if they were one topic, and then extracted a correlation matrix. This topic correlation matrix was converted to a network, as presented in Figure 2. There, each of the topic clusters serves as a node in the network (N ¼ 48), and the strengths of their connections (i.e., correlations) are represented as weighted edges in the network, with different colors for the subdiscipline designations identified. In visualizing the network, we used the Kamada-Kawai algorithm, which is a standard force-directed algorithm commonly used to visualize weighted social networks. 9 As demonstrated in Figure 2, we can see some expected patterns based on common knowledge about subdiscipline differentiations and specialization trends in communication science. For instance, within the political communication subdiscipline, topic clusters such as "conflicts and protest movements" are strongly connected to "mediatization" and "mass political behavior." Within the media psychology subdiscipline, topics concerning "message processing," "emotion and affect," and "persuasion" are strongly connected to each other, as well as relatively well-connected to general "attitude" topics (which form a separate cluster of their own). In the health communication subdiscipline, topic clusters such as "health behavioral change and campaign effects" are rather loosely connected within the remainder of the subdiscipline (such as "public health and health literacy"), while we also see some newer topics such as "virtual reality and health" or "addiction and problematic use of ICTs." Note: The Kamada-Kawai algorithm is used to generate network visualization. It is a standard force-directed algorithm commonly used to visualize social networks for weighted graphs, producing esthetically pleasing, interpretable graphs. However, due to the complexity of the resulting visualization of our network, we strongly recommend examining the interactive version of the figure, which can be found at: https://osf.io/2m8zw. Also see Table SA1 in the Online Appendix for detailed information regarding Identified topic lists (with labels) and their subdiscipline designations.

Among the identified clusters, "public/governance" (e.g., public, government, governance, policy, citizen, etc.) was most strongly connected to other topics (weighted vertex degree centrality ¼ 1.35), followed by "third-person perception" (¼ 1.31), and "online media and ICTs" (¼ 1.24). In contrast, "interpersonal deception" (¼ .17), "psychology of survey response" (¼ .08), and "linguistic interactions" (.02) topic clusters were least connected to the rest of the discipline's research topics. In terms of betweenness (which signals the importance of a given topic in connecting otherwise disconnected topic clusters with each other), topics such as "media entertainment" (weighted normalized betweenness ¼ .34), "race & intergroup" (¼ .33), and "ethics & morality" (¼ .30) scored highest. In contrast, "journalism and media coverage," "education and teaching," and "addiction and problematic use of ICTs" scored lowest (all normalized betweenness ¼ 0), suggesting that such topics are mostly confined to one particular subdiscipline (e.g., journalism, children and media, and health communication, respectively).

In sum, these first results provide some face validity of our approach. However, Figure 2 also suggests that there appear to be no visibly recognizable patterns of fissure across different subdisciplines in the field, even though there exist many niche topics that are tightly intertwined with different parts of the subdiscipline linkage network.

Moving from the qualitative inspection, and in order to answer RQ2, we conducted a series of simulation-based inferences examining several network-level indices of the observed network: (a) degree distributions, (b) average path distance, (c) transitivity (i.e., a global clustering coefficient), (d) the average pairwise node connectivity, and (e) graph modularity; we will further explain and illustrate each of these indices later. RQ2 asked which topological properties of scientific knowledge-per Figure 1 above-best describe the subdisciplinary linkage patterns in communication science, and relatedly, how much fragmentation there is compared to such prototypical network structures.

We first examine (a) the degree distribution of the observed network. This is to test whether our network (i.e., interconnections among topics/topic clusters within and across subdisciplines) can be characterized as a "scale-free" network, a network topology implied in the traditional disciplinary model. In a scale-free network, a node can attract a greater number of additional nodes in a self-reinforcing manner by virtue of its number of existing connections; the resulting degree distribution in this network structure would be highly centralized onto a few "influential" nodes (Baraba ´si & Albert, 1999; also see Newman, 2001). This is typically tested by examining the cumulative degree distribution of a network on the double logarithmic (i.e., log-log) scale (Moody, 2004).

For this test, we randomly generate 1,000 networks conforming to each of the four topologies (as in Figure 1), and then compare the cumulative degree distributions from each against the empirically observed subdisciplinary linkage network. Figure 3 shows the results of this test, where a black dashed line represents the observed cumulative degree distribution, whereas colored lines with confidence bands represent the means and 95% CIs, respectively, of the simulated distributions from four prototypical network topologies (N ¼ 1,000 for each topology). Using a nonparametric test of equality of two cumulative distributions (the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test), we reject the null hypothesis of equality of the observed versus simulated degree distributions, D ¼ .487, p ¼ .001, only against the scale-free network topology (Figure 1a). This means that the observed degree distribution in our subdisciplinary linkage network (as in Figure 1) is unlikely to be structured upon the core-periphery topology implied in the traditional disciplinary model. For the rest of network topologies, however, we fail to reject the equality of the observed vs. simulated degree distributions (Figure 1b-d: all p > .05). Here, not finding a power law distribution (as in Figure 1a) would falsify a core-periphery model, while the opposite does not necessarily mean that a given network is only consistent with a core-periphery model. Indeed, many competing mechanisms other than preferential attachment mechanism can generate the power law distribution (Moody, 2004). In addition, this does not directly answer how "fragmented" a given network really is, but instead merely suggesting that our network is unlikely to be the same as the core-periphery topology. For this reason, we turn to the next set of tests.

Having found evidence against the scale-free structure (therefore rejecting the traditional disciplinary model), we next examine network "fragmentation" directly by considering the following: (b) average path distance, (c) transitivity, (d) average pairwise node connectivity, and (e) graph modularity. The average path distance is the average geodesic distance among all pairs of nodes, where geodesic distance is the shortest possible path between two given nodes. 10 The average path distance tends to increase as the network becomes more fragmented. The transitivity (i.e., a global clustering coefficient) is a measure of the network's tendency to form densely connected subgroups (Friedkin, 1984 ;Watts & Strogatz, 1998) by looking at the density of triangles. The mean pairwise node connectivity represents the average number of in-between nodes that must be removed in order to disconnect the randomly chosen pair of nodes in the network. Lastly, modularity (Newman, 2006) measures the strength of division of a network into "modules" (or communities) by comparing edge densities in given clusters compared to edge densities between clusters. All four measures, therefore, provide a reasonable indication of the overall cohesion (i.e., the opposite of "fragmentation") of the network (Beineke et al., 2002;Torrents & Ferraro, 2015). Using a simulation-based inferential test (N ¼ 1,000 for each of the topologies), Figure 4 presents the comparison between the network features of our observed network (in red dotted lines) and that of the randomly generated networks (the means and 95% CIs).

Figure 4 reveals several interesting findings regarding the likely topological structure behind our subdisciplinary linkage network. First, Figure 4a indicates that the mean weighted distance of the observed network (obs ¼ 1.7986) is most similar to and slightly higher than that of the structural cohesion model (M ¼ 1.7094, 95% CIs ¼ [1.7002, 1.7208]), and a little smaller than the small-world model (M ¼ 2.0478, 95% CIs ¼ [1.9679, 2.1471]). We found that the mean weighted distance from the typical scale-free network (M ¼ 1.6819, 95% CIs ¼ [1.6771, 1.6867]) was also similar to that of the observed network; however, our test of cumulative degree distribution (per Figure 3) makes it clear that the traditional disciplinary scale-free network model is unlikely to provide a good topology for describing our subdiscipline linkage network. Likewise, a typical "fragmented network" scores substantially higher (M ¼ 2.3957, 95% CIs ¼ [2.2044, 2.8881]) on its mean distance than our subdisciplinary linkage network. This suggests that the fragmented network structure is not a good candidate for explaining our empirical subdisciplinary linkage network.

In line with this result, our observed network appears to be the most similar to the structural cohesion model in terms of its mean pairwise connectivity (Figure 4c: obs ¼ 7.5496 vs. simulated M ¼ 7.9507, 95% CIs ¼ [7.6009, 8.2651]) and graph modularity (Figure 4d: obs ¼ .1420 vs. simulated M ¼ .2010, 95% CIs ¼ [.1573, .2425]). For both of the metrics, again the most dissimilar network topology was the fragmented model.

In terms of graph transitivity (Figure 4b), we find that the overall transitivity is substantially higher-approximately twice as likely-in the observed network than in the structural cohesion model (obs ¼ .5164 vs. M ¼ .2764, 95% CIs ¼ [.2527, .3028]). Given that a clustering coefficient signifies the opposite of fragmentation (i.e., a tendency to form a dense subgraph), this indicates that the observed connections among subdisciplines are indeed heavily clustered, being about twice as high as would be expected in the structural cohesion model. In contrast, the observed transitivity value is more similar to that of the small-world model (obs ¼ .5164 vs. M ¼ .6118, 95% CIs ¼ [.5805, .6401]). Indeed, in order for a given network structure to be a small-world (Watts & Strogatz, 1998), a network should exhibit a shorter or at least equal degree of mean distance, but a much higher degree of clustering (i.e., transitivity) than is expected in a simple random (i.e., structural cohesion network). This essentially suggests that our observed network features empirically fall between a simple random network (i.e., structural cohesion model) and a typical small-world network.

Overall, the results of Figure 4 suggest that subdisciplinary linkage patterns in communication science are no more "substantially fragmented" than would be expected in an (equal-sized) cohesion model in terms of its mean distance and average connectivity, and are indeed very tightly connected when considering the average clustering (i.e., transitivity). Furthermore, it suggests that the structure of subdisciplinary linkage patterns also manifests certain characteristics of a smallworld model (especially in terms of transitivity) at the same time. Nevertheless, it remains far from a plural world (i.e., fragmented) model, a structure which many previous commentaries regarding the status of the discipline would imply. The fragmented model appears to be most dissimilar from the observed subdiscipline linkage network in terms of all of the metrics examined here, as the differences between simulated values from the fragmented model and observed values are greater than any other topologies.

In the health communication subdiscipline, topic clusters such as "health behavioral change and campaign effects" are rather loosely connected within the remainder of the subdiscipline (such as "public health and health literacy"), while we also see some newer topics such as "virtual reality and health" or "addiction and problematic use of ICTs." Note: The Kamada-Kawai algorithm is used to generate network visualization. It is a standard force-directed algorithm commonly used to visualize social networks for weighted graphs, producing esthetically pleasing, interpretable graphs. However, due to the complexity of the resulting visualization of our network, we strongly recommend examining the interactive version of the figure, which can be found at: https://osf.io/2m8zw. Also see Table SA1 in the Online Appendix for detailed information regarding Identified topic lists (with labels) and their subdiscipline designations.

Among the identified clusters, "public/governance" (e.g., public, government, governance, policy, citizen, etc.) was most strongly connected to other topics (weighted vertex degree centrality ¼ 1.35), followed by "third-person perception" (¼ 1.31), and "online media and ICTs" (¼ 1.24). In contrast, "interpersonal deception" (¼ .17), "psychology of survey response" (¼ .08), and "linguistic interactions" (.02) topic clusters were least connected to the rest of the discipline's research topics. In terms of betweenness (which signals the importance of a given topic in connecting otherwise disconnected topic clusters with each other), topics such as "media entertainment" (weighted normalized betweenness ¼ .34), "race & intergroup" (¼ .33), and "ethics & morality" (¼ .30) scored highest. In contrast, "journalism and media coverage," "education and teaching," and "addiction and problematic use of ICTs" scored lowest (all normalized betweenness ¼ 0), suggesting that such topics are mostly confined to one particular subdiscipline (e.g., journalism, children and media, and health communication, respectively).

In sum, these first results provide some face validity of our approach. However, Figure 2 also suggests that there appear to be no visibly recognizable patterns of fissure across different subdisciplines in the field, even though there exist many niche topics that are tightly intertwined with different parts of the subdiscipline linkage network.

Moving from the qualitative inspection, and in order to answer RQ2, we conducted a series of simulation-based inferences examining several network-level indices of the observed network: (a) degree distributions, (b) average path distance, (c) transitivity (i.e., a global clustering coefficient), (d) the average pairwise node connectivity, and (e) graph modularity; we will further explain and illustrate each of these indices later. RQ2 asked which topological properties of scientific knowledge-per Figure 1 above-best describe the subdisciplinary linkage patterns in communication science, and relatedly, how much fragmentation there is compared to such prototypical network structures.

We first examine (a) the degree distribution of the observed network. This is to test whether our network (i.e., interconnections among topics/topic clusters within and across subdisciplines) can be characterized as a "scale-free" network, a network topology implied in the traditional disciplinary model. In a scale-free network, a node can attract a greater number of additional nodes in a self-reinforcing manner by virtue of its number of existing connections; the resulting degree distribution in this network structure would be highly centralized onto a few "influential" nodes (Baraba ´si & Albert, 1999; also see Newman, 2001). This is typically tested by examining the cumulative degree distribution of a network on the double logarithmic (i.e., log-log) scale (Moody, 2004).

For this test, we randomly generate 1,000 networks conforming to each of the four topologies (as in Figure 1), and then compare the cumulative degree distributions from each against the empirically observed subdisciplinary linkage network. Figure 3 shows the results of this test, where a black dashed line represents the observed cumulative degree distribution, whereas colored lines with confidence bands represent the means and 95% CIs, respectively, of the simulated distributions from four prototypical network topologies (N ¼ 1,000 for each topology). Using a nonparametric test of equality of two cumulative distributions (the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test), we reject the null hypothesis of equality of the observed versus simulated degree distributions, D ¼ .487, p ¼ .001, only against the scale-free network topology (Figure 1a). This means that the observed degree distribution in our subdisciplinary linkage network (as in Figure 1) is unlikely to be structured upon the core-periphery topology implied in the traditional disciplinary model. For the rest of network topologies, however, we fail to reject the equality of the observed vs. simulated degree distributions (Figure 1b-d: all p > .05). Here, not finding a power law distribution (as in Figure 1a) would falsify a core-periphery model, while the opposite does not necessarily mean that a given network is only consistent with a core-periphery model. Indeed, many competing mechanisms other than preferential attachment mechanism can generate the power law distribution (Moody, 2004). In addition, this does not directly answer how "fragmented" a given network really is, but instead merely suggesting that our network is unlikely to be the same as the core-periphery topology. For this reason, we turn to the next set of tests.

Having found evidence against the scale-free structure (therefore rejecting the traditional disciplinary model), we next examine network "fragmentation" directly by considering the following: (b) average path distance, (c) transitivity, (d) average pairwise node connectivity, and (e) graph modularity. The average path distance is the average geodesic distance among all pairs of nodes, where geodesic distance is the shortest possible path between two given nodes. 10 The average path distance tends to increase as the network becomes more fragmented. The transitivity (i.e., a global clustering coefficient) is a measure of the network's tendency to form densely connected subgroups (Friedkin, 1984 ;Watts & Strogatz, 1998) by looking at the density of triangles. The mean pairwise node connectivity represents the average number of in-between nodes that must be removed in order to disconnect the randomly chosen pair of nodes in the network. Lastly, modularity (Newman, 2006) measures the strength of division of a network into "modules" (or communities) by comparing edge densities in given clusters compared to edge densities between clusters. All four measures, therefore, provide a reasonable indication of the overall cohesion (i.e., the opposite of "fragmentation") of the network (Beineke et al., 2002;Torrents & Ferraro, 2015). Using a simulation-based inferential test (N ¼ 1,000 for each of the topologies), Figure 4 presents the comparison between the network features of our observed network (in red dotted lines) and that of the randomly generated networks (the means and 95% CIs).

Figure 4 reveals several interesting findings regarding the likely topological structure behind our subdisciplinary linkage network. First, Figure 4a indicates that the mean weighted distance of the observed network (obs ¼ 1.7986) is most similar to and slightly higher than that of the structural cohesion model (M ¼ 1.7094, 95% CIs ¼ [1.7002, 1.7208]), and a little smaller than the small-world model (M ¼ 2.0478, 95% CIs ¼ [1.9679, 2.1471]). We found that the mean weighted distance from the typical scale-free network (M ¼ 1.6819, 95% CIs ¼ [1.6771, 1.6867]) was also similar to that of the observed network; however, our test of cumulative degree distribution (per Figure 3) makes it clear that the traditional disciplinary scale-free network model is unlikely to provide a good topology for describing our subdiscipline linkage network. Likewise, a typical "fragmented network" scores substantially higher (M ¼ 2.3957, 95% CIs ¼ [2.2044, 2.8881]) on its mean distance than our subdisciplinary linkage network. This suggests that the fragmented network structure is not a good candidate for explaining our empirical subdisciplinary linkage network.

In line with this result, our observed network appears to be the most similar to the structural cohesion model in terms of its mean pairwise connectivity (Figure 4c: obs ¼ 7.5496 vs. simulated M ¼ 7.9507, 95% CIs ¼ [7.6009, 8.2651]) and graph modularity (Figure 4d: obs ¼ .1420 vs. simulated M ¼ .2010, 95% CIs ¼ [.1573, .2425]). For both of the metrics, again the most dissimilar network topology was the fragmented model.

In terms of graph transitivity (Figure 4b), we find that the overall transitivity is substantially higher-approximately twice as likely-in the observed network than in the structural cohesion model (obs ¼ .5164 vs. M ¼ .2764, 95% CIs ¼ [.2527, .3028]). Given that a clustering coefficient signifies the opposite of fragmentation (i.e., a tendency to form a dense subgraph), this indicates that the observed connections among subdisciplines are indeed heavily clustered, being about twice as high as would be expected in the structural cohesion model. In contrast, the observed transitivity value is more similar to that of the small-world model (obs ¼ .5164 vs. M ¼ .6118, 95% CIs ¼ [.5805, .6401]). Indeed, in order for a given network structure to be a small-world (Watts & Strogatz, 1998), a network should exhibit a shorter or at least equal degree of mean distance, but a much higher degree of clustering (i.e., transitivity) than is expected in a simple random (i.e., structural cohesion network). This essentially suggests that our observed network features empirically fall between a simple random network (i.e., structural cohesion model) and a typical small-world network.

Overall, the results of Figure 4 suggest that subdisciplinary linkage patterns in communication science are no more "substantially fragmented" than would be expected in an (equal-sized) cohesion model in terms of its mean distance and average connectivity, and are indeed very tightly connected when considering the average clustering (i.e., transitivity). Furthermore, it suggests that the structure of subdisciplinary linkage patterns also manifests certain characteristics of a smallworld model (especially in terms of transitivity) at the same time. Nevertheless, it remains far from a plural world (i.e., fragmented) model, a structure which many previous commentaries regarding the status of the discipline would imply. The fragmented model appears to be most dissimilar from the observed subdiscipline linkage network in terms of all of the metrics examined here, as the differences between simulated values from the fragmented model and observed values are greater than any other topologies.
