sections.paper_id: Song et al. - 2020 - Less Fragmented Than We Thought Toward Clarification of a Subdisciplinary Linkage in Communication
paragraphs: In comparing the observed subdisciplinary linkage relative to the four prototypical networks with different topological properties, our analysis revealed that the subdisciplinary linkage in communication science is less fragmented than is often assumed. First and foremost, the structure of linkage patterns across subdisciplines in communication science has been found to be something of a combination of a small-world model and a structural cohesion model, yet far from a plural, fragmented model, at least within the context of what communication scholars "write" and publish within the discipline's top journals. We did not find any evidence supporting the notion of "plural worlds" that are severely disconnected and whose members have nothing in common, as implied in popular narratives concerning the fragmentation of the field. Instead, we have identified a surprisingly encouraging degree of cohesion and reachability across different subfields of communication science research. Echoing a recent contribution on this topic (e.g., Gu ¨nther & Domahidi, 2017), this result suggests that the common belief of the "fractured" status of the discipline may be overstated.

Second, and in line with common perceptions, our results show that there is no single, dominant research theme or subdiscipline that constitutes a "core" of communication science. Rather, we have found that the subdisciplinary linkage patterns can be characterized as a relatively even mix of different niche topics and specialty areas. Here, we may note that certain research topics and specialty areas do form a distinctive subdiscipline that is easily distinguishable from others. However, there appear to be no visibly recognizable fissures across different subdisciplines, and the structure of the entire subdisciplinary linkage network cannot be characterized in terms of "fragmented," disconnected archipelagoes. While the lack of common theoretical knowledge still bears on the question of what the core identity of the discipline really is, communication science does not appear to be suffering from the fragmentation of multiple exclusive research domains.

Although our analysis has provided some tentative answers vis-a `-vis the so-called "fragmentation thesis," there are several limitations to consider in our description of the field. First, we limited our attention to a set of articles that were published in specific "top journals" from 2010 until 2019. Due to some technical difficulties, we also had to exclude certain journals and articles (e.g., Journal of Advertising Research and International Journal of Communication; see Online Appendix for details), which may have biased our results. However, our case selection and screening criteria were unlikely to be highly correlated with the actual content of those excluded journals and articles, especially given that we incorporated a good mix of more general outlets (such as JoC, HCR, or CR) and a broader representation of more specialized outlets dedicated to established subdisciplines. Nevertheless, by definition, this does not cover the whole scope of the communication research field. By inevitably excluding more specialized outlets and topic-specific journals, it is possible that our mapping of communication subdisciplines missed more niche, specialized subfields that have only recently started to emerge (e.g., communication neuroscience, human-machine communication, computational communication, etc.), and that we may be classifying those emerging fields as a part of the existing subdisciplines. Regardless, we have found that the "fragmentation thesis" is not supported based on topical connections, at least within the discipline's top journals examined here. Considering such reachability and connectivity within the discipline's "major" journals, we think that the overall picture regarding the state of the field would have changed little even if we had included those niche subfields, as long as the major journals continue to bridge and integrate different areas of communication science. This vividly highlights the critical role of more general, broad journal outlets (such as JoC or CR) in representing and connecting diverse subfields with different specialty areas, promoting a broader integration of the field as a whole.

Second, our analysis did not consider citation patterns or information about authors (as typically done in bibliometric analyses). While we believe-as noted in the above section-that such information alone cannot answer whether the field of communication science is indeed fragmented or not, a fuller understanding of scientific knowledge production and integration would be rendered possible by linking authors (i.e., production) and citation patterns (i.e., consumption) with the actual "content" of articles. While such an approach requires considerably different sources of data (e.g., full information about all authors as well as detailed citation patterns at the time of publication) and analytical strategies, jointly considering such characteristics along with the actual contents of scientific articles (as presented here) would enable us to better understand the underlying factors that shape specialization trends within the communication field.

Despite such limitations, our analysis reveals some important implications regarding the status of the discipline, and subdisciplinary specialization trends more generally. First, we found that the communication discipline as a whole has no particular single intellectual idea and theoretical tradition that rigidly defines "communication science" (e.g., Waisbord, 2019). However, we have also noted that there are no visibly recognizable patterns of differentiation across different subdisciplines in the field, in the sense that there exist many niche topics that are integrated into different parts of the subdiscipline linkage network. While intellectually plural and fuzzy, different subtopics of communication studies continue to be intertwined into different areas of the research field, while the linkage network as a whole exhibits a surprising degree of reachability and connectivity at the global scale. Unfortunately, our results cannot directly speak to possible underlying factors that shape such surprisingly high-connectivity patterns. Nevertheless, studies on disciplinary differentiation/integration in other fields of social science often suggest that a scientific field with little theoretical consensus (such as education, sociology, and psychology) frequently exhibit similar trends to those found in our analysis: a coexistence of many niche specialty areas and loose internal boundaries of subdivisions (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006).

Second and more importantly, the results of our "reality check" provide little reason to believe that there actually exists a severe fragmentation of the discipline that warrants the possibility of the demise of the field as a whole. Rather, high permeability and a lack of clear internal subdisciplinary boundaries render certain ideas and theories in communication science easily transferable, as they can be creatively applied to multiple research contexts. This provides a means of reconciling apparent differentiation (e.g., specialization) and theoretical integration (e.g., structural cohesion) simultaneously, a topological structure of which we have found in our analysis and in other studies (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody & Light, 2006). Nevertheless, future studies would benefit from a comparative perspective that employs a similar analytical approach across different academic contexts and over time. A computational approach of inductively modeling "topics" (as we have done here) has a particular inferential advantage, as it enables the identification of similar concepts and ideas that use different labels across different subfields of communication studies.

Lastly, the question remains regarding what cultivates the apparent perception of a "fragmented field" in the first place. Focusing on mass communication, Wahl-Jorgensen has concluded that "the choices we make about what disciplinary territory to annex are profoundly normative" ( (2012 , p. 573). Looking at our results against the backdrop of such argumentation, it seems that rather than the "discipline" per se being fragmented, the perception of communication scholars may be fragmented regarding the normative boundaries of the field (e.g., Waisbord, 2019). Indeed, many researchers may be missing the forest for the trees, proving incapable of seeing the bigger picture beyond their idiosyncratic connections with other subdisciplines. At the same time, there are no strategic incentives to look beyond their specialty areas when researchers produce scientific knowledge. Therefore, an interesting avenue for future research may be to more directly compare the self-perception of communication as a discipline with the perception of fragmentation, and furthermore to identify which factors shape the relationship between the two in order to better evaluate the state and self-perception of communication as a discipline.

1. Here, we refer to "interdisciplinarity" and "multidisciplinarity" as two opposing concepts, yet the exact definitions across scholars are highly ambiguous and therefore inconsistent. Indeed, Klein (2010) and Choi and Pak (2006) acknowledge such heterogeneity in meanings. Nevertheless, for the sake of simplicity of argument, we frame the two terms as generally opposing concepts (following the most extensive, up-to-date conceptual analysis by Choi & Pak (2006); also see MacInnis & Folkes ( 2009)), even though the terms are used somewhat interchangeably in the literature. 2. While Moody (2004) characterizes the "fractured" topology of knowledge production as the small-world model (Watts, 1999;Watts & Strogatz, 1998), others argue that this does not necessarily imply a highly fractured network structure (Carolan, 2008). 3. The use of full-texts may on the contrary limit the scope of our analysis due to data availability. We return to this point later in the Discussion section. 4. This means that journals need not be listed as one of the top 10 consistently throughout the entire period. For instance, Communication Monographs was listed first in 2010 and second in 2011, yet it was not listed among the top 10 for the rest of the period. Our focus on the highest ranking journals was to ensure a good mixture of broad, general outlets as well as relatively specialized outlets. 5. In the initial list of journals, Journal of Advertising Research was excluded because fulltext access was not provided by the authors' institution. International Journal of Communication was excluded as the full-text version was only based on a PDF format. Likewise, any articles from remaining journals that were only available in PDF format were excluded, specifically: Journal of Advertising (until the entire year of 2012, volume 41 issue 4), International Journal of Advertising (until the entire year of 2014, volume 33 issue 4), Science Communication (until September 2011, volume 33 issue 3), New Media & Society (until the entire year of 2011, volume 18 issue 8), and International Journal of Press/Politics (until July 2011, volume 16 issue 3). This was necessary because our methods of parsing full-texts required access to a plain HTML version of each article. We are not aware of any reliable method to properly parse all metadata and full-texts from a PDF file. 6. The literature generally recommends setting the number of topic K fairly high, as any duplicated topics can be grouped together, whereas "hybrid" topics (the combination of two or more distinct substantive topics) cannot be disaggregated later (Walter and Ophir, 2019).

7. In cases where all authors agreed that there was a particular subdiscipline for a topic cluster, we assigned the respective subdiscipline to a given topic cluster. In cases where there were more than two substantive subdisciplines for which a given topic cluster was considered, we assigned multiple subdisciplines. In cases where we could not agree, or we all agreed that there was no particular subdiscipline, such topics were identified as "no particular subdiscipline." 8. This is because such methods are not specific to a particular subdiscipline, and therefore may risk us drawing an incorrect conclusion about the state of the discipline, especially due to their high connectivity to multiple subdisciplines. 9. The Kamada-Kawai algorithm can be applied straightforwardly to weighted graphs whose edge lengths have to reflect their weights. This generally produces interpretable, esthetically pleasing visualizations for weighted graphs. 10. In a weighted network, the distance is often interpreted as the "cost" of traveling between nodes, implying that a higher weight between a given pair of nodes means greater "distance." However, given that the weight between a given pair of nodes in this network means a "stronger" connection (i.e., correlation), we take the inverse of 1 minus edge weights, in turn, normalized by its average value, in calculating distance measures (using igraph's distances function). The resulting normalized mean distance has a range of 0-1, a higher value representing greater "distance," given the average edge strengths between a random pair of nodes in a given network.
sections.text:
In comparing the observed subdisciplinary linkage relative to the four prototypical networks with different topological properties, our analysis revealed that the subdisciplinary linkage in communication science is less fragmented than is often assumed. First and foremost, the structure of linkage patterns across subdisciplines in communication science has been found to be something of a combination of a small-world model and a structural cohesion model, yet far from a plural, fragmented model, at least within the context of what communication scholars "write" and publish within the discipline's top journals. We did not find any evidence supporting the notion of "plural worlds" that are severely disconnected and whose members have nothing in common, as implied in popular narratives concerning the fragmentation of the field. Instead, we have identified a surprisingly encouraging degree of cohesion and reachability across different subfields of communication science research. Echoing a recent contribution on this topic (e.g., Gu ¨nther & Domahidi, 2017), this result suggests that the common belief of the "fractured" status of the discipline may be overstated.

Second, and in line with common perceptions, our results show that there is no single, dominant research theme or subdiscipline that constitutes a "core" of communication science. Rather, we have found that the subdisciplinary linkage patterns can be characterized as a relatively even mix of different niche topics and specialty areas. Here, we may note that certain research topics and specialty areas do form a distinctive subdiscipline that is easily distinguishable from others. However, there appear to be no visibly recognizable fissures across different subdisciplines, and the structure of the entire subdisciplinary linkage network cannot be characterized in terms of "fragmented," disconnected archipelagoes. While the lack of common theoretical knowledge still bears on the question of what the core identity of the discipline really is, communication science does not appear to be suffering from the fragmentation of multiple exclusive research domains.

Although our analysis has provided some tentative answers vis-a `-vis the so-called "fragmentation thesis," there are several limitations to consider in our description of the field. First, we limited our attention to a set of articles that were published in specific "top journals" from 2010 until 2019. Due to some technical difficulties, we also had to exclude certain journals and articles (e.g., Journal of Advertising Research and International Journal of Communication; see Online Appendix for details), which may have biased our results. However, our case selection and screening criteria were unlikely to be highly correlated with the actual content of those excluded journals and articles, especially given that we incorporated a good mix of more general outlets (such as JoC, HCR, or CR) and a broader representation of more specialized outlets dedicated to established subdisciplines. Nevertheless, by definition, this does not cover the whole scope of the communication research field. By inevitably excluding more specialized outlets and topic-specific journals, it is possible that our mapping of communication subdisciplines missed more niche, specialized subfields that have only recently started to emerge (e.g., communication neuroscience, human-machine communication, computational communication, etc.), and that we may be classifying those emerging fields as a part of the existing subdisciplines. Regardless, we have found that the "fragmentation thesis" is not supported based on topical connections, at least within the discipline's top journals examined here. Considering such reachability and connectivity within the discipline's "major" journals, we think that the overall picture regarding the state of the field would have changed little even if we had included those niche subfields, as long as the major journals continue to bridge and integrate different areas of communication science. This vividly highlights the critical role of more general, broad journal outlets (such as JoC or CR) in representing and connecting diverse subfields with different specialty areas, promoting a broader integration of the field as a whole.

Second, our analysis did not consider citation patterns or information about authors (as typically done in bibliometric analyses). While we believe-as noted in the above section-that such information alone cannot answer whether the field of communication science is indeed fragmented or not, a fuller understanding of scientific knowledge production and integration would be rendered possible by linking authors (i.e., production) and citation patterns (i.e., consumption) with the actual "content" of articles. While such an approach requires considerably different sources of data (e.g., full information about all authors as well as detailed citation patterns at the time of publication) and analytical strategies, jointly considering such characteristics along with the actual contents of scientific articles (as presented here) would enable us to better understand the underlying factors that shape specialization trends within the communication field.

Despite such limitations, our analysis reveals some important implications regarding the status of the discipline, and subdisciplinary specialization trends more generally. First, we found that the communication discipline as a whole has no particular single intellectual idea and theoretical tradition that rigidly defines "communication science" (e.g., Waisbord, 2019). However, we have also noted that there are no visibly recognizable patterns of differentiation across different subdisciplines in the field, in the sense that there exist many niche topics that are integrated into different parts of the subdiscipline linkage network. While intellectually plural and fuzzy, different subtopics of communication studies continue to be intertwined into different areas of the research field, while the linkage network as a whole exhibits a surprising degree of reachability and connectivity at the global scale. Unfortunately, our results cannot directly speak to possible underlying factors that shape such surprisingly high-connectivity patterns. Nevertheless, studies on disciplinary differentiation/integration in other fields of social science often suggest that a scientific field with little theoretical consensus (such as education, sociology, and psychology) frequently exhibit similar trends to those found in our analysis: a coexistence of many niche specialty areas and loose internal boundaries of subdivisions (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody, 2004;Moody & Light, 2006).

Second and more importantly, the results of our "reality check" provide little reason to believe that there actually exists a severe fragmentation of the discipline that warrants the possibility of the demise of the field as a whole. Rather, high permeability and a lack of clear internal subdisciplinary boundaries render certain ideas and theories in communication science easily transferable, as they can be creatively applied to multiple research contexts. This provides a means of reconciling apparent differentiation (e.g., specialization) and theoretical integration (e.g., structural cohesion) simultaneously, a topological structure of which we have found in our analysis and in other studies (e.g., Carolan, 2008;Moody & Light, 2006). Nevertheless, future studies would benefit from a comparative perspective that employs a similar analytical approach across different academic contexts and over time. A computational approach of inductively modeling "topics" (as we have done here) has a particular inferential advantage, as it enables the identification of similar concepts and ideas that use different labels across different subfields of communication studies.

Lastly, the question remains regarding what cultivates the apparent perception of a "fragmented field" in the first place. Focusing on mass communication, Wahl-Jorgensen has concluded that "the choices we make about what disciplinary territory to annex are profoundly normative" ( (2012 , p. 573). Looking at our results against the backdrop of such argumentation, it seems that rather than the "discipline" per se being fragmented, the perception of communication scholars may be fragmented regarding the normative boundaries of the field (e.g., Waisbord, 2019). Indeed, many researchers may be missing the forest for the trees, proving incapable of seeing the bigger picture beyond their idiosyncratic connections with other subdisciplines. At the same time, there are no strategic incentives to look beyond their specialty areas when researchers produce scientific knowledge. Therefore, an interesting avenue for future research may be to more directly compare the self-perception of communication as a discipline with the perception of fragmentation, and furthermore to identify which factors shape the relationship between the two in order to better evaluate the state and self-perception of communication as a discipline.
